ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for mochi; A0EJW (rice cake, glutinous rice-based) used as closest defensible analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for mochi as a specific food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard piece of mochi weighs approximately 45 g, which is a typical single-serve portion as sold in Japanese confectionery shops and supermarkets.

nutrient_source.title: Mochi — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Mochi (CR ID F11100014). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for commercial mochi (glutinous rice flour + sugar + water) was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 78 is estimated from glutinous rice (white, cooked) as the closest approved analogous food reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), which lists glutinous rice GI = 78 (glucose reference). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (78 × 46.44 × 0.45) / 100 = 16.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (23.89 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.65 g/100 g), driving insulin-mediated sodium retention and blood pressure elevation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit mochi to one small piece (30–40 g) on rare occasions, as its very high sugar content (23.89 g/100 g) with minimal fibre (1.65 g/100 g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention and independently raises cardiovascular risk; the 132 mg/100 g sodium load further compounds this concern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high sugar (23.89 g/100 g, exceeding the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling) and high GI (~78) from glutinous rice flour base, with low fibre (1.65 g/100 g) providing minimal glycaemic buffering
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid mochi or restrict intake to a very small piece (20–30 g) on rare occasions, as its sugar content of 23.89 g/100 g exceeds the 20 g/100 g hard glycaemic gate, its estimated GI of 78 (high) causes rapid postprandial glucose spikes, and its low fibre (1.65 g/100 g) provides negligible glycaemic buffering; blood glucose monitoring is strongly advised if consumed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar content (23.89 g/100 g) contributing to dyslipidaemia and triglyceride elevation; low saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g) is a mitigating factor
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume mochi sparingly, as its high free sugar content (23.89 g/100 g) can elevate serum triglycerides and contribute to dyslipidaemia; however, its very low fat content (0.5 g/100 g, saturated fat ~0.1 g/100 g) means direct lipid-raising risk from fat is minimal, and the primary concern is sugar-driven metabolic burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar (23.89 g/100 g) and refined glutinous rice flour base with minimal fibre (1.65 g/100 g) promote pro-inflammatory mediators; no positive anti-inflammatory factor present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid mochi, as its high sugar content (23.89 g/100 g) and refined carbohydrate base with minimal fibre (1.65 g/100 g) promote inflammatory mediators and may trigger RA flares; no anti-inflammatory factors (omega-3, polyphenols, whole-food fibre >3 g, fermented components) are present to offset this risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mochi is a traditional Japanese sweet rice cake made from glutinous rice flour (mochiko), sugar, and water. The dough is steamed or microwaved and then kneaded until smooth and elastic, producing a characteristically soft, chewy, and slightly sticky confection with a mild sweetness. Mochi has a dense, pillowy texture that is distinct from other rice-based snacks. It is commonly eaten plain, dusted with kinako (roasted soybean flour) or cocoa powder, or filled with sweet red bean paste (anko), ice cream, or other sweet fillings. Mochi is deeply embedded in Japanese culinary culture, traditionally consumed during the New Year (Oshogatsu) and other festivals, and is now widely available in Singapore and Southeast Asia as a popular snack. Nutritionally, mochi is energy-dense at 206 kcal/100 g, with a high carbohydrate content of 46.44 g/100 g and a very high sugar content of 23.89 g/100 g. Its protein (3.82 g/100 g) and fat (0.5 g/100 g) are low, and dietary fibre is minimal at 1.65 g/100 g. The glutinous rice flour base confers a high glycaemic index (~78), making mochi one of the most glycaemia-spiking snack foods. It is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension due to its high sugar load and low fibre content. Sodium is moderate at 132 mg/100 g. Mochi is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and dairy-free in its plain form.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in mochi?
What is the GI of mochi?
Can I eat mochi if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Foodo Singapore database (CR ID F11100014) at confidence 0.95. GI is estimated from the closest approved analogous food (glutinous rice, GI 78) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed source, with estimation basis documented. All mandatory fields are complete.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for mochi are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry (F11100014) or in USDA FoodData Central for this specific product formulation as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat estimated at 0.1 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central entry for mochi (FDC ID 1101626) which reports negligible saturated fat consistent with the near-zero total fat of 0.5 g/100 g. GI of 78 is an analogous estimate from glutinous rice (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct mochi GI measurement was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving (45 g piece): GL = (78 × 46.44 × 0.45) / 100 = 16.3 (high). Mochi is naturally gluten-free despite being a grain-based product, as glutinous rice contains no gluten protein. The food is suitable for vegan and halal diets in its plain form (no animal-derived ingredients). FoodEx2 code A0EJW used as closest defensible analogue for glutinous rice cake; exact mochi code not confirmed in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
